segments.title: A war aimed at breaking the League
segments.summary: The Copenhagen operation is introduced as a British effort to break the Second League of Armed Neutrality, not a campaign for Danish territory.
segments.body: The central issue is a league that Britain sees as protecting French interests while Britain is already at war with France and Spain. The operation is therefore not presented as an attempt to conquer Danish territory. Its purpose is to break a maritime coalition before its fleets and economic pressure can reinforce Britain's enemies. Dr. Clarke frames the 1801 crisis as naval diplomacy backed by force, with combat serving a limited political objective.

evidence.note: The introduction identifies the attack and the League of Armed Neutrality as the cause of the conflict.

segments.title: Denmark-Norway's impossible strategic choice
segments.summary: Crown Prince Frederick faced pressure from France and Russia on one side and Britain on the other, with Copenhagen controlling the Baltic approaches.
segments.body: Denmark-Norway could appear to choose Britain and risk pressure or invasion from France and Russia, or join the league and provoke Britain. Crown Prince Frederick understood that either course could lead to war, yet Denmark did not fully mobilize its fleet, fortify Copenhagen, or summon allied forces forward. Its capital's position beside the Danish straits made that indecision unusually consequential. Baltic access mattered to fleets, commerce, and coalition strategy, so Denmark-Norway could not behave as though its alignment were a purely diplomatic choice.

evidence.note: The lecture explains Frederick's choices and Copenhagen's strategic position.

segments.title: Neutral convoys, blockade, and Baltic naval stores
segments.summary: The dispute joined neutral-convoy rights to Britain's need to inspect commerce, maintain its blockade, and secure Scandinavian supplies for the Royal Navy.
segments.body: Britain treated neutral convoys carrying French ships or goods as a practical threat to its blockade and prize-court system. British warships normally detained doubtful vessels and let prize courts decide whether ships or cargoes should be released. Denmark and other neutral powers instead claimed that their convoy protection should prevent British searches, even when French-flagged ships or French-bound goods were involved. The problem became more acute because Scandinavian naval stores were vital to British sea power. A league that could shelter enemy commerce, weaken the blockade, and restrict naval supplies was therefore not merely a legal argument about neutral flags.

evidence.note: The transcript connects convoy disputes, French protection, blockade enforcement and Scandinavian supplies.

segments.title: Why the League could fall like dominoes
segments.summary: The earlier league worked around fleet movements and winter ice; the 1800 version left Denmark exposed before stronger Baltic fleets could act together.
segments.body: Catherine II's earlier league had considered the seasonal rhythm of northern fleets and arranged its pressure when the members could support one another. The Second League formed as winter was closing the northern Baltic bases. Denmark's waters opened sooner than Russia's, leaving the smallest exposed member reachable before the stronger Russian fleet was free of ice. That transformed a supposed united front into a sequence of separate targets. Britain could strike the first domino and use the resulting momentum to weaken the whole coalition.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke contrasts the timing of the first and second leagues and describes the domino logic.

segments.title: Tsar Paul turns neutrality into a coalition threat
segments.summary: The Second League of Armed Neutrality included Russia, Denmark-Norway, Prussia and Sweden, but Paul I's alignment with France undermined its claim to neutrality.
segments.body: The league brought Russia, Denmark-Norway, Prussia, and Sweden together during the War of the Second Coalition. Its members could present it as protection for neutral commerce, but Tsar Paul I also aligned Russia with France. Britain's leaders consequently saw Russian policy as linked to their French enemy rather than as impartial protection of shipping. The coalition also threatened British access to Baltic supplies. That combination explains why London treated the league as a potential hostile naval bloc rather than a detached legal arrangement.

evidence.note: The transcript names the league members and explains why Paul I's French alignment alarmed Britain.

segments.title: The fleet as diplomatic cover
segments.summary: Britain first used a fleet to press for an agreement, hoping its presence would give Denmark-Norway room to disengage from French and Russian pressure.
segments.body: The British approach mixed coercion with a proposed settlement over convoy rules. A smaller mission in August 1800 had opened talks with Denmark, and London hoped to extend agreed rules to Sweden and other Baltic states. The visible fleet also gave Denmark-Norway a possible explanation for making concessions under pressure rather than appearing voluntarily to abandon France and Russia. It demonstrated that Britain could support states threatened around the Baltic. The difficulty of feeding and sustaining large continental armies in northern Europe made mobile sea power an especially useful counterweight.

evidence.note: The lecture describes the 1800 mission, diplomatic intent, and the logistical limits on a French land intervention.

segments.title: The race before the Baltic thaw
segments.summary: Britain had to act before ice released Russian ships that could combine with Swedish and Danish forces into a formidable Baltic fleet.
segments.body: The potential combined fleet shaped British planning even if the largest estimate of 123 ships of the line was a worst-case scenario. A much smaller Baltic concentration of forty or fifty ships could still become dangerous if it joined Dutch forces or acted while Franco-Spanish fleets pressed from the south. London feared losing control of the Channel under simultaneous pressure. Britain also wanted to act before French or Russian troops reinforced Denmark. Striking the league while its fleets and armies remained divided was therefore the operational logic behind the race against the thaw.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses the ice-bound Russian fleet and the danger of combined Baltic forces.

segments.title: Parker's diplomacy and Nelson's fighting role
segments.summary: Hyde Parker was selected for a controlled, diplomatic campaign while Nelson supplied the aggressive fighting capacity if diplomacy failed.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke contrasts Hyde Parker's methodical temperament and obedience to orders with Horatio Nelson's forceful operational style. Parker was suited to a campaign intended to leave time for negotiation and let league members reconsider their position. Nelson, the victor of the Nile, supplied the aggressive fighting capacity if diplomacy failed. Placing Parker in overall command kept the limited political aim prominent. Making Nelson second-in-command ensured that the fleet still had a commander able to force the issue at Copenhagen.

evidence.note: The transcript compares Parker and Nelson and explains why Parker held overall command.

segments.title: Danish command misreads Britain's objective
segments.summary: Denmark's commanders prepared a harbour battle without centring the British requirement to move bomb vessels within range of Copenhagen and break the League.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that Denmark's command appointments and planning did not match the opponent or the political problem. Against Nelson, the victor of the Nile, much of the Danish fleet was converted into stationary batteries without a clear plan for using the remaining mobile ships. The British did not need to destroy every Danish vessel or capture the city. They needed to clear a route for bomb vessels to threaten Copenhagen and compel Denmark-Norway to leave the League. Once that limited objective is recognized, preserving an untouched northern force while leaving the southern defence to bear the decisive attack looks less like success and more like a failure to contest the operation.

evidence.note: The lecture critiques Danish command choices, identifies the bomb-vessel objective, and explains the intended diplomatic domino effect.

segments.title: Denmark's static defence and unused mobile ships
segments.summary: Danish ships and batteries created a formidable-looking harbour defence, but command arrangements left mobile ships unused when a flanking attack might have mattered.
segments.body: The Danish plan combined moored ships, floating batteries, shore batteries, and a small group of vessels kept rigged and ready for sea. In theory, the mobile force could wait until Nelson's ships anchored and then attack individual British vessels from the other side, reproducing the double-sided pressure used at the Nile. No such sortie took place. Command changes, uncertainty over who should lead it, and the decision to keep the reserve facing Parker's northern division left Nelson's flank unchallenged. Dr. Clarke argues that a coordinated use of the available ships could have made the attack far harder or even physically impossible in its chosen form.

evidence.note: The lecture explains the Danish battery system and the unrealized opportunity for mobile ships to attack Nelson's anchored line.

segments.title: A limited British squadron with a coercive mission
segments.summary: The British did not send an overwhelming battle fleet; their useful leverage lay in a small attacking squadron and bomb vessels able to threaten Copenhagen.
segments.body: Parker retained two second rates and six third rates in the northern reserve while Nelson took twelve nominal ships of the line into the southern channel. Three of Nelson's ships grounded, so only nine reached the attack. The force included 64-gun ships and two smaller fourth rates rather than an overwhelming concentration of Britain's newest capital ships. Its most important coercive leverage came from seven bomb vessels supported by frigates and smaller craft. Bringing those bomb vessels within range of Copenhagen could turn a limited tactical success into direct political pressure on the Danish government.

evidence.note: The transcript lists the British force, notes three ships failed to engage, and explains the bomb-vessel threat.

segments.title: Nelson's southern anchoring attack
segments.summary: Nelson approached from the south rather than the expected north, anchoring ships successively opposite the Danish line to concentrate fire without overextending his force.
segments.body: The Danish defence expected the principal attack from the north, but Nelson approached through the southern channel after inspecting the difficult waters. Each British ship sailed past the vessels already engaged and anchored by the stern opposite the next Danish position. This brought the line into action successively without allowing it to overextend. Dr. Clarke compares the effect to a walking barrage moving up the defensive line. The navigation remained hazardous: Agamemnon, Russell, and Bellona grounded before they could take their intended places.

evidence.note: The transcript explains the unexpected southern approach, successive anchoring and three grounded British ships.

segments.title: Gunnery experience and a failing reinforcement system
segments.summary: The anchored fleets exchanged fire while Denmark's attempts to reinforce ships from shore batteries depleted the crews needed to keep those batteries firing.
segments.body: The fleets anchored roughly a cable apart and exchanged broadsides, leaving little room for manoeuvre once the line was formed. Danish crews fought with determination, and low floating batteries added fire that the British had not fully noticed before the action. Reinforcements were then rowed out from the shore batteries to vessels already under heavy fire. That weakened the fixed defences without reliably restoring the battered ships, and eventually untrained volunteers were sent into the same system. The fully crewed reserve ships to the north remained inactive while Parker's heavier division approached, leaving Nelson's attack unchallenged from its flank.

evidence.note: The lecture describes the firefight, shore-to-ship reinforcements and inactive northern Danish ships.

segments.title: Isis and Monarch survive concentrated Danish fire
segments.summary: Prøvesteenen nearly overwhelmed the 50-gun HMS Isis before HMS Désirée and HMS Polyphemus intervened, while HMS Monarch suffered under concentrated Danish fire.
segments.body: At about 1 p.m., Prøvesteenen's fire placed the 50-gun HMS Isis in serious danger. The frigate HMS Désirée raked the Danish position with assistance from HMS Polyphemus, preventing it from finishing Isis. Elsewhere, HMS Monarch was damaged by the combined fire of Holstein and Sjælland. These ship-level episodes show that the Danish line could inflict severe punishment even though its command system failed to coordinate the wider defence. The passage also foreshadows Holstein's later distinction as the only captured Danish vessel retained for British service.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the danger to Isis, Désirée and Polyphemus attacking Prøvesteenen, and Monarch taking fire from Holstein and Sjælland.

segments.title: Why Nelson ignored the recall signal
segments.summary: Parker's signal gave Nelson discretion to disengage; Nelson acknowledged it but continued the close action, producing the famous blind-eye episode.
segments.body: Parker saw distress signals from grounded British ships and used the recall signal to give Nelson a lawful way to disengage if he could no longer continue. The transcript presents it as discretionary cover rather than an unavoidable command to retreat. Nelson acknowledged the signal but did not repeat it, making the famous remark about having a right to be blind sometimes. His subordinate Thomas Graves kept Nelson's close-action signal prominent while placing the recall where other ships could not readily see it. Captain Edward Riou, unable to see Nelson's flagship, obeyed Parker literally, withdrew his frigate force past heavier fortress fire, and was killed.

evidence.note: The transcript recounts Parker's signal, Nelson's response, Graves's handling of signals and Riou's withdrawal.

segments.title: From battle to coercive negotiation
segments.summary: As the Danish line fell silent, Nelson brought bomb vessels closer and used a flag of truce to turn naval victory into negotiations with Crown Prince Frederick.
segments.body: Experienced British gunnery progressively silenced most of the Danish line by about 2 p.m. The shore batteries had also been depleted by sending their crews out as reinforcements, allowing British bomb vessels to move closer to Copenhagen. Nelson then sent a Danish-speaking officer under a flag of truce to Crown Prince Frederick. His message offered to spare Denmark if resistance ceased but warned that captured floating batteries might otherwise be burned with their defenders aboard. The ensuing exchange produced a 24-hour ceasefire and shifted the operation from battle back to diplomacy. The damaged Danish flagship Dannebrog exploded after the agreement, while several battered British ships grounded as they withdrew, underscoring how costly the tactical victory had been.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the shift in firing, bomb vessels, Nelson's message, the ceasefire, Dannebrog's explosion, and British ships grounding during withdrawal.

segments.title: The cost of Copenhagen's static defence
segments.summary: Danish-Norwegian losses remain uncertain because many volunteers lacked naval experience, while Britain recorded 963 killed and wounded and destroyed most captured Danish ships.
segments.body: Danish-Norwegian losses cannot be reconstructed precisely because many volunteers were not consistently recorded before or after the battle. Estimates for those killed, wounded, and captured range widely, while the British recorded 963 killed and wounded. The lecture stresses that patriotic courage could not substitute for trained crews able to load, aim, and fight almost automatically under fire. Two Danish ships sank, one exploded, and twelve were captured. Britain expected that further Baltic fighting might follow and could not spare prize crews, so it burned eleven captured vessels and retained only Holstein, later commissioned as HMS Nassau.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses uncertain Danish losses, British casualties, training, captured ships and Holstein's fate.

segments.title: Paul's death ends the diplomatic crisis
segments.summary: News of Tsar Paul's assassination allowed Denmark to regard the League as finished, settle with Britain, and end the Baltic operation before a wider fleet war developed.
segments.body: The League was tied so closely to Paul I that news of his assassination gave Denmark room to negotiate without openly rupturing relations with Russia. A settlement was reached, after which Parker sailed toward Karlskrona and induced the Swedish fleet to return to port. His reluctance to push farther into the eastern Baltic led Britain to recall him with honour and transfer command to Nelson. Nelson then advanced to Reval while leaving ships to watch Sweden. By the time he arrived, the thawed Russian fleet had departed for Kronstadt and negotiations to end the League were already under way, making further action unnecessary.

evidence.note: The transcript links Paul's death to Danish negotiations, Parker's recall, Nelson's Baltic movement and the end of operations.

segments.title: Remembering naval historian James Goldrick
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke pauses after the Copenhagen narrative to remember James Goldrick as an exceptional naval historian who actively welcomed and encouraged younger scholars.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke marks the death of James Goldrick with a personal tribute to both his scholarship and his generosity. He recalls Goldrick making time for younger historians, joining discussions such as Bilge Pumps, and encouraging new participants at naval and maritime history conferences. A story from a Greenwich conference shows Goldrick easing the pressure on a young speaker with humour before a formidable audience. The tribute argues that later generations can produce new leading scholars without simply replacing the distinctive kindness and mentorship of those who have died. Opening the video here offers a glimpse of how the naval-history community reproduces itself through encouragement as well as published research.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke remembers James Goldrick's scholarship, mentorship, humour, and influence on younger naval historians.
